item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had not initially picked up on what Drewbear noticed but even without that I liked it. Though, I think it could do with an edit to tone down the reveal. Leaving a little to the imagination could benefit this greatly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was good, I feel like it was based on a specific killer. It makes me think of BTK. Was that the case or was it coincidental?

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably very traumatic for anyone to "read" the entire book. One wonders what the effects would be if you were to "play" it backwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like it. Those last pages, and the implication that the victim/body is once again sensate are what makes it work for me. One of my big problems with "the dead rising from their graves" trope is that digging with just your hands is hard, especially if your hands/arms are immobile by being trapped/buried. It's why people trapped under rubble after earthquakes or whatever are so rarely able to dig themselves out. So now the body/victim from the book is sensate again, and stuck in their grave forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was waiting for this one. Will read after class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this, and will add that the researcher note was pretty trite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has been placed up for review, please come look. It needs more attention.

Like others in the discussion, I feel it's too predictable. I was waiting for a curveball that never came.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I appreciate most about this one is the well placed expungement and the disturbingness of the whole SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you think scratch-and-sniff is pretty neat, huh? Well, try this.

I particularly like the note at the end. "We understand it, now quit traumatizing test subjects with it! …it mucks up the results for the gibbering-horror-what-crazies-you-to-death tests."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. I thought it is well written and held my attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think it was all that predictable. I only realized what was going on when I got to the chloroform part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't say it holds my interest as much as I'd like it to…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. I don't really mind it's a bit predictable- it's well written and fairly interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for catching the non-metric thing, fixed now. Fair enough on the other points; I'll think on the predictability of this and see if I can't do something to punch it up in the morning. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how to vote on this one. Tone is alright and the concept is interesting, but the effects just feel too "predictable" as it goes on, there's sudden use of non-metric units at the end, and it's a pretty basic "memories of a traumatic event imprinted on an object" story like the ones I heard all the time on haunted house "documentaries" when I was a kid. Page 133 seems to describe knocking someone out with a chloroform-soaked rag, which really only works that way on TV - in real life, you're more liable to either do nothing at all, or kill the person.

No vote for now - I don't like it, and I don't hate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP going up. All suggestions and feedback appreciated in advance.
